name: I had a bad experience with schema before—how can I safely try "schema for AI search" again without breaking things?
description: After a bad schema experience, reset with a simple, well-documented rollout on a few key pages using core types. Validate each step and expand gradually once yo

mainEntity.name: I had a bad experience with schema before—how can I safely try "schema for AI search" again without breaking things?

acceptedAnswer.text:
If you’ve had a bad schema experience in the past, restart with a narrow, well-documented implementation focused on a few key types and pages.
The goal is to rebuild trust with something your team can understand and maintain.

Key factors:
- Overly complex, opaque setups are hard to debug and often go stale.
- Limited scope (Organization, LocalBusiness, Article, FAQPage) is easier to validate.
- Clear internal documentation prevents future confusion.
- Incremental expansion lets you spot issues early.

Practically, strip away any legacy markup you can’t explain, then reintroduce schema starting with your homepage, main location pages, and a couple of FAQ hubs.
Validate each change and track basic performance indicators.
A fresh, simpler implementation often performs better than trying to salvage an overly tangled legacy.

short_answer:"After a bad schema experience, reset with a simple, well-documented rollout on a few key pages using core types. Validate each step and expand gradually once you’re confident everything is accurate and maintainable."
acceptedAnswer.description: After a bad schema experience, reset with a simple, well-documented rollout on a few key pages using core types. Validate each step and expand gradually once you’re confident everything is accurate and maintainable.
